headline: Johor Voted 40-16 to Add Five Appointed Assemblymen After Legal Adviser Said No
context: On 7 May 2026, Johor's state assembly voted 40-16 to allow up to five appointed members in addition to 56 elected ones. The state's own Legal Adviser had warned in 2025 that this was inconsistent with the Federal Constitution.

cards.big: Johor's state assembly voted 40-16 to add up to five appointed members. The split was the 2022 election, replayed on the floor.
cards.sub: Barisan Nasional's 40-seat majority cleared the two-thirds bar. Eight months earlier, the state's own Legal Adviser called it inconsistent with the Federal Constitution.

cards.big: Johor's own State Legal Adviser called appointed assemblymen inconsistent with the Federal Constitution in September 2025.
cards.sub: The state passed the amendment in May 2026 anyway. Eighth Schedule §4(1) describes state assemblies as having 'elected members.' No court has ruled.

cards.big: Pahang took four years between enacting and appointing. Johor compressed both into one day.
cards.sub: Pahang's five 2024 appointees all carried party labels (UMNO, MCA, Amanah, PKR, plus a tahfiz-school founder). The state's stated rationale was 'persons of calibre,' not technocrats. All five were men.

cards.big: The same day the bill passed, nine women's-rights groups demanded all five seats go to women.
cards.sub: Johor's elected assembly is 25% women, already Malaysia's highest. Five women appointees would push it past the 30% Beijing Platform benchmark. None of Pahang's 2024 appointees were women.

cards.big: The question is not whether the amendment is constitutional. It is what it means when a state passes a law its own legal adviser called inconsistent eight months earlier.
Imagine a council that, before its next election, lets the current chair appoint five new voting members.
cards.sub: The new members can vote on budgets and contracts but cannot become chair. The election still happens, with five extra votes already locked in.

cards.big: Sarawak grew its assembly from 82 seats to 99 last year, all elected. Johor chose to grow by five appointments before voters next vote.
